Section 2.5 — Continuous random variables¶

This notebook contains all the code examples from Section 2.5 Continuous random variables of the No Bullshit Guide to Statistics.

Topics covered in this notebook:

  • Definitions of continuous random variables
  • Examples of random variables
  • Probability calculations
  • Computer models for random variables
    • Overview of scipy.stats methods
  • Real-world example to demo probability applications
  • Discussion
    • Bulk and tails of a the normal distribution

Notebook setup¶

We'll start by importing the Python modules we'll need for this notebook.

In [1]:
Copied! 
# load Python modules
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
# load Python modules import numpy as np import pandas as pd import seaborn as sns import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
In [2]:
Copied! 
# Figures setup
sns.set_theme(
    context="paper",
    style="whitegrid",
    palette="colorblind",
    rc={'figure.figsize': (7,2)},
)
%config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'retina'
# Figures setup sns.set_theme( context="paper", style="whitegrid", palette="colorblind", rc={'figure.figsize': (7,2)}, ) %config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'retina'
In [3]:
Copied! 
%pip install -q ministats
%pip install -q ministats
Note: you may need to restart the kernel to use updated packages.
In [4]:
Copied! 
from ministats import plot_pdf_and_cdf
from ministats import plot_pdf_and_cdf
In [5]:
Copied! 
# set random seed for repeatability
np.random.seed(3)
# set random seed for repeatability np.random.seed(3)

Definitions¶

Random variables¶

  • random variable $X$: a quantity that can take on different values.
  • outcome: a particular value $\{X = x\}$ or range of values $\{a \leq X \leq b\}$ that can occur as a result of observing the random variable $X$.
  • sample space $\mathcal{X}$: describes the set of all possible outcomes of the random variable.
  • $f_X$: the probability distribution function is a function that assigns probabilities to the different outcome in the sample space of a random variable. The probability distribution function of the random variable $X$ is a function of the form $f_X: \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}$.
  • $F_X$: the cumulative distribution function (CDF) tells us the probability of an outcome less than or equal to a given value: $F_X(b) = Pr(\{ X \leq b \})$.
  • $F_X^{-1}$: the inverse cumulative distribution function computes contains the information about the quantiles of the probability distribution. The value $F_X^{-1}(q)=x_q$ tells how far you need to go in the sample space so that the event $\{ X \leq x_q \}$ contains a proportion $q$ of the total probability: $\Pr(\{ X \leq x_q \})=q$.
  • $\mathbb{E}_X[w]$: the expected value of the function $w(X)$ computes the average value of $w(X)$ computed for all the possible values of the random variable $X$.

Example 1: Uniform distribution¶

The uniform distribution $\mathcal{U}(0,1)$ is described by the following probability density function:

$$ p_U(u) = \begin{cases} 1 & \mathrm{for}\ 0 \le u \le 1, \\ 0 & \mathrm{for}\ u<0\ \mathrm{or}\ u>1. \end{cases} $$

where $U$ is the name of the random variable and $u$ are particular values it can take on.

The above equation describes tells you how likely it is to observe $\{U=u\}$. For a uniform distribution $\mathcal{U}(0,1)$, each $u$ between 0 and 1 is equally likely to occur, and values of $u$ outside this range have zero probability of occurring.

Computer simulation

  • The continuous uniform family of distribution $\mathcal{U}(\alpha,\beta)$, which assigns equal probabilities to all outcomes in the interval $[\alpha,\beta]$. To create a computer model for a continuous uniform distribution, use the code uniform(alpha,beta), where alpha and beta are two floats.

We'll introduce computer models for random variables is Section 2.1.5 — Computer models for random variables below, but since we're looking at a notebook, we can show a little preview of the calculations you'll learn by the end of the section.

In [6]:
Copied! 
# define the computer model `rvU` for the random variable U
from scipy.stats import uniform
rvU = uniform(0, 1)

# use `quad` function to integrate rvU.pdf between 0.2 and 0.5
from scipy.integrate import quad
quad(rvU.pdf, 0.2, 0.5)[0]
# define the computer model `rvU` for the random variable U from scipy.stats import uniform rvU = uniform(0, 1) # use `quad` function to integrate rvU.pdf between 0.2 and 0.5 from scipy.integrate import quad quad(rvU.pdf, 0.2, 0.5)[0]
Out[6]:
0.3
In [7]:
Copied! 
plot_pdf_and_cdf(rvU, b=0.7, xlims=[-0.2,1.2], rv_name="U");
plot_pdf_and_cdf(rvU, b=0.7, xlims=[-0.2,1.2], rv_name="U");

Example 2: Normal distribution¶

A random variable $N$ with a normal distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma)$ is described by the probability density function:

$$ f_N(n) = \tfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{\small -\tfrac{(n-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}. $$

The mean $\mu$ (the Greek letter mu) and the standard deviation $\sigma$ (the Greek letter sigma) are called the parameters of the distribution.

The math notation $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma)$ is used to describe the whole family of normal probability distributions, and $K \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_K=1000, \sigma_K=10)$ is a particular instance of the distribution with mean $\mu_K = 1000$ and standard deviation $\sigma_K = 10$.

The code example below shows the calculation of the probability $\Pr\!\left( \{ 980 \leq K \leq 990 \} \right)$, which corresponds to the integral $\int_{k=980}^{k=990} f_K(k) dk$.

In [8]:
Copied! 
# define the computer model `rvK` for the random variable K
from scipy.stats import norm
muK = 1000
sigmaK = 10
rvK = norm(muK, sigmaK)

# use `quad` function to integrate rvK.pdf between 980 and 990
quad(rvK.pdf, 980, 990)[0]
# define the computer model `rvK` for the random variable K from scipy.stats import norm muK = 1000 sigmaK = 10 rvK = norm(muK, sigmaK) # use `quad` function to integrate rvK.pdf between 980 and 990 quad(rvK.pdf, 980, 990)[0]
Out[8]:
0.13590512198327784
In [9]:
Copied! 
plot_pdf_and_cdf(rvK, b=1010, rv_name="K");
plot_pdf_and_cdf(rvK, b=1010, rv_name="K");

Calculations with random variables¶

Example 1: mean and variance of the uniform distribution¶

Mean¶

\begin{align*} \mu_U = \mathbb{E}_U[U] &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u f_U(u) \; du \\ &= \int_0^1 u f_U(u) \; du \\ &= \int_0^1 u \cdot 1 \; du = \tfrac{1}{2}. \end{align*}

In [10]:
Copied! 
# from sympy import symbols, integrate
# u = symbols('u')
# integrate(u * 1, (u,0,1))
# from sympy import symbols, integrate # u = symbols('u') # integrate(u * 1, (u,0,1))
In [11]:
Copied! 
from scipy.integrate import quad

def u_times_fU(u):
	return u * 1

quad(u_times_fU, 0, 1)[0]
from scipy.integrate import quad def u_times_fU(u): return u * 1 quad(u_times_fU, 0, 1)[0]
Out[11]:
0.5

So the mean is $\mu_U = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$.

Variance¶

The formula for the variance is

$$ \sigma_U^2 = \mathbb{E}_U\!\left[ (U-\mu_U)^2 \right] = \int_0^1 (u- \tfrac{1}{2})^2 \cdot f_U(u) \; du. $$

In [12]:
Copied! 
# from sympy import symbols, integrate
# u = symbols('u')
# integrate( (u-1/2)**2 * 1, (u,0,1) )
# from sympy import symbols, integrate # u = symbols('u') # integrate( (u-1/2)**2 * 1, (u,0,1) )
In [13]:
Copied! 
def u_minus_muU_squared(u):
    return (u-0.5)**2

quad(u_minus_muU_squared, 0, 1)[0]
def u_minus_muU_squared(u): return (u-0.5)**2 quad(u_minus_muU_squared, 0, 1)[0]
Out[13]:
0.08333333333333333

So the variance of $U$ is $\sigma_U^2 = 0.08\overline{3} = \frac{1}{12}$.

We can compute the standard deviation $\sigma_U$ by taking the square root of the variance.

In [14]:
Copied! 
import numpy as np
np.sqrt(0.0833333333333333)
import numpy as np np.sqrt(0.0833333333333333)
Out[14]:
0.2886751345948128

Example 2: mean and variance of a normal distribution¶

$$ p_K(k) = \tfrac{1}{\sigma_K\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\tfrac{(k-\mu_K)^2}{2\sigma_K^2}} = \tfrac{1}{10\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\tfrac{(k-1000)^2}{2\cdot10^2}}. $$

In [15]:
Copied! 
import numpy as np

muK = 1000
sigmaK = 10

def fK(k):
    z = (k - muK) / sigmaK
    C = sigmaK * np.sqrt(2*np.pi)
    return 1 / C * np.exp(-1/2 * z**2)
import numpy as np muK = 1000 sigmaK = 10 def fK(k): z = (k - muK) / sigmaK C = sigmaK * np.sqrt(2*np.pi) return 1 / C * np.exp(-1/2 * z**2)

The mean of $K$ is

In [16]:
Copied! 
from scipy.integrate import quad

def k_times_fK(k):
    return k * fK(k)

quad(k_times_fK, 0, 2000)[0]
from scipy.integrate import quad def k_times_fK(k): return k * fK(k) quad(k_times_fK, 0, 2000)[0]
Out[16]:
999.9999999999997

The standard deviation of $K$ is

In [17]:
Copied! 
def k_minus_meanK_sq_times_fK(k):
    return (k - 1000)**2 * fK(k)

quad(k_minus_meanK_sq_times_fK, 0, 2000)[0]
def k_minus_meanK_sq_times_fK(k): return (k - 1000)**2 * fK(k) quad(k_minus_meanK_sq_times_fK, 0, 2000)[0]
Out[17]:
100.00000000000004

Skewness and kurtosis¶

In [18]:
Copied! 
rvK.stats("s")
rvK.stats("s")
Out[18]:
0.0
In [19]:
Copied! 
rvK.stats("k")
rvK.stats("k")
Out[19]:
0.0

Computer models for random variables¶

  • <model>: the family of probability distributions
  • <params>: parameters of the model—specific value of the control knobs we choose in the general family of distributions to create a particular distribution
  • <model>(<params>): a particular instance of probability model created by choosing a model family <model> and the model parameters <params>.
    • example 1: uniform family of distribution $\mathcal{U}(\alpha,\beta)$ with parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$.
    • example 2: normal family of distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma)$ with parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma$.
  • $\sim$: math shorthand symbol that stands for "is distributed according to." For example $X \sim \texttt{model}(\theta)$ means the random variable $X$ is distributed according to model instance $\texttt{model}$ and parameters $\theta$.
In [20]:
Copied! 
from scipy.stats import norm

# create a normal random variable with mean 1000 and std 10
rvK = norm(1000, 10)
from scipy.stats import norm # create a normal random variable with mean 1000 and std 10 rvK = norm(1000, 10)
In [21]:
Copied! 
type(rvK)
type(rvK)
Out[21]:
scipy.stats._distn_infrastructure.rv_continuous_frozen
In [22]:
Copied! 
## see all attributes and methods:
# [attr for attr in dir(rvK) if "__" not in attr]
## see all attributes and methods: # [attr for attr in dir(rvK) if "__" not in attr]

Plotting the probability density function¶

In [23]:
Copied! 
ks = np.linspace(950, 1050, 1000)
fKs = rvK.pdf(ks)
sns.lineplot(x=ks, y=fKs, label="pdf of $K$");
ks = np.linspace(950, 1050, 1000) fKs = rvK.pdf(ks) sns.lineplot(x=ks, y=fKs, label="pdf of $K$");

The cumulative distribution is the integral of the probability density function:

$$ F_K(b) = \textrm{Pr}(\{K \leq b\}) = \int_{-\infty}^b f_K(k) \, dk. $$

In [24]:
Copied! 
ks = np.linspace(950, 1050, 1000)
FKs = rvK.cdf(ks)
sns.lineplot(x=ks, y=FKs, label="CDF of $K$");
ks = np.linspace(950, 1050, 1000) FKs = rvK.cdf(ks) sns.lineplot(x=ks, y=FKs, label="CDF of $K$");

Properties of the distribution¶

In [25]:
Copied! 
rvK.mean()
rvK.mean()
Out[25]:
1000.0
In [26]:
Copied! 
rvK.std()
rvK.std()
Out[26]:
10.0
In [27]:
Copied! 
rvK.var()
rvK.var()
Out[27]:
100.0
In [28]:
Copied! 
rvK.std() # == np.sqrt(rvK.var())
rvK.std() # == np.sqrt(rvK.var())
Out[28]:
10.0
In [29]:
Copied! 
rvK.median()
rvK.median()
Out[29]:
1000.0
In [30]:
Copied! 
rvK.support()
rvK.support()
Out[30]:
(-inf, inf)

Computing probabilities¶

Suppose you want to compute the probability of the outcome $\{ a \leq K \leq b \}$ for the random variable $K$.

In [31]:
Copied! 
# Pr({980 < N < 1020}) = integral of f_N between 980 and 1020
quad(rvK.pdf, 980, 1020)[0]
# Pr({980 < N < 1020}) = integral of f_N between 980 and 1020 quad(rvK.pdf, 980, 1020)[0]
Out[31]:
0.9544997361036411
In [32]:
Copied! 
# Pr({980 < N < 1020}) = F_N(1020) - F_N(980)
rvK.cdf(1020) - rvK.cdf(980)
# Pr({980 < N < 1020}) = F_N(1020) - F_N(980) rvK.cdf(1020) - rvK.cdf(980)
Out[32]:
0.9544997361036416

Computing quantiles¶

The inverse question is to find the interval $(-\infty, k_q]$ that contains proportion $q$ of the total probability.

First quartile¶

The first quartile ($q=0.25$ quantile) is located at $F_K^{-1}(0.25)$

In [33]:
Copied! 
rvK.ppf(0.25)
rvK.ppf(0.25)
Out[33]:
993.2551024980392

We can verify that $\Pr({K \leq 993.2551024980392)}) = 0.25$

In [34]:
Copied! 
rvK.cdf(993.2551024980392)
rvK.cdf(993.2551024980392)
Out[34]:
0.24999999999999895

Second quartile¶

In [35]:
Copied! 
rvK.ppf(0.5)
rvK.ppf(0.5)
Out[35]:
1000.0

Third quartile¶

In [36]:
Copied! 
rvK.ppf(0.75)
rvK.ppf(0.75)
Out[36]:
1006.7448975019608

Left tail¶

In [37]:
Copied! 
rvK.ppf(0.05)
rvK.ppf(0.05)
Out[37]:
983.5514637304852

Right tail¶

In [38]:
Copied! 
rvK.ppf(0.95)
rvK.ppf(0.95)
Out[38]:
1016.4485362695148

Computing confidence intervals¶

To compute a 90% confidence interval for the random variable $K$, we can call the rvK.ppf() twice.

In [39]:
Copied! 
[rvK.ppf(0.05), rvK.ppf(0.95)]
[rvK.ppf(0.05), rvK.ppf(0.95)]
Out[39]:
[983.5514637304852, 1016.4485362695148]

Generating random observations¶

Let's say you want to generate $n=10$ observations from the random variable $N$. You can do this by calling the method rvN.rvs(n).

In [40]:
Copied! 
np.random.seed(45)

ksample = rvK.rvs(10)
ksample.round(2)
np.random.seed(45) ksample = rvK.rvs(10) ksample.round(2)
Out[40]:
array([1000.26, 1002.6 ,  996.05,  997.96,  987.28,  974.03, 1002.9 ,
        991.27, 1003.94, 1009.35])

Sample mean¶

In [41]:
Copied! 
np.mean(ksample)
np.mean(ksample)
Out[41]:
996.5642932112244

Sample standard deviation¶

In [42]:
Copied! 
np.std(ksample, ddof=1)
np.std(ksample, ddof=1)
Out[42]:
10.177891977273164

Computing expectations¶

Suppose the distributor accepts only bottles contain between 980 ml and 1020 ml, and you'll receive a receive payment of $\$2$ for each bottle. Bottles outside that range get rejected and you don't get paid for them.

In [43]:
Copied! 
def payment(k):
    if 980 <= k and k <= 1020:
        return 2
    else:
        return 0
def payment(k): if 980 <= k and k <= 1020: return 2 else: return 0
In [44]:
Copied! 
# get paid if in spec
payment(1005)
# get paid if in spec payment(1005)
Out[44]:
2
In [45]:
Copied! 
# don't get paid if out of spec
payment(1025)
# don't get paid if out of spec payment(1025)
Out[45]:
0
In [46]:
Copied! 
# expected value of payment
rvK.expect(payment, lb=0, ub=2000)
# expected value of payment rvK.expect(payment, lb=0, ub=2000)
Out[46]:
1.9089994711961735

Visually speaking, only parts of the probability mass of the random variable "count" towards the payment, the subset of the values inside the yellow region shown below.

In [47]:
Copied! 
ks = np.linspace(900, 1100, 1000)
payments = [payment(k)/50 for k in ks]

sns.lineplot(x=ks, y=rvK.pdf(ks))
sns.lineplot(x=ks, y=payments, label="payment(k)")
ks = np.linspace(900, 1100, 1000) payments = [payment(k)/50 for k in ks] sns.lineplot(x=ks, y=rvK.pdf(ks)) sns.lineplot(x=ks, y=payments, label="payment(k)")
Out[47]:
<Axes: >

Useful probability formulas¶

Multivariable expectation¶

Independent, identically distributed random variabls¶

Discussion¶

Bulk of the normal distribution¶

How much of the total probability "weight" lies within $n$ standard deviations of the mean?

$$ \textrm{Pr}(\mu_K - n\sigma_K \leq K \leq \mu_K + n\sigma_K) \ = \ p_n. $$

In [49]:
Copied! 
from scipy.stats import norm
rvK = norm(1000, 10)
from scipy.stats import norm rvK = norm(1000, 10)
In [50]:
Copied! 
from scipy.integrate import quad

muK = rvK.mean()    # mean of the random variable rvK
sigmaK = rvK.std()  # standard deviation of rvK

for n in [1, 2, 3]:
    I_n = [muK - n*sigmaK, muK + n*sigmaK]
    p_n = quad(rvK.pdf, I_n[0], I_n[1])[0]
    print(f"p_{n} = Pr( K in {I_n} ) = {p_n:.3f}")
from scipy.integrate import quad muK = rvK.mean() # mean of the random variable rvK sigmaK = rvK.std() # standard deviation of rvK for n in [1, 2, 3]: I_n = [muK - n*sigmaK, muK + n*sigmaK] p_n = quad(rvK.pdf, I_n[0], I_n[1])[0] print(f"p_{n} = Pr( K in {I_n} ) = {p_n:.3f}")
p_1 = Pr( K in [990.0, 1010.0] ) = 0.683
p_2 = Pr( K in [980.0, 1020.0] ) = 0.954
p_3 = Pr( K in [970.0, 1030.0] ) = 0.997

The code below highlights the interval $I_n$ and computes the probability $p_n$. Change the value of the variable n to get different plots.

In [51]:
Copied! 
from ministats import calc_prob_and_plot

n = 2  # number of standard deviations around the mean

# values of x in the interval 𝜇 ± n𝜎 = [𝜇-n𝜎, 𝜇+n𝜎]
I_n = [muK - n*sigmaK, muK + n*sigmaK]
p_n, _ = calc_prob_and_plot(rvK, I_n[0], I_n[1])
p_n
from ministats import calc_prob_and_plot n = 2 # number of standard deviations around the mean # values of x in the interval 𝜇 ± n𝜎 = [𝜇-n𝜎, 𝜇+n𝜎] I_n = [muK - n*sigmaK, muK + n*sigmaK] p_n, _ = calc_prob_and_plot(rvK, I_n[0], I_n[1]) p_n
Out[51]:
0.9544997361036411

Try changing the value of the variable n to 1 or 3 in the above code cell.

Tails of the normal distribution¶

We're often interested in tail ends of the distribution, which contain the unlikely events.

In [52]:
Copied! 
muK = rvK.mean()    # mean of the random variable rvK
sigmaK = rvK.std()  # standard deviation of rvK

for n in [1, 2, 3]:
    # compute the probability in the left tail (-∞,𝜇-n𝜎]
    x_l = muK - n*sigmaK
    p_l = quad(rvK.pdf, rvK.ppf(0.0000000001), x_l)[0]
    # compute the probability in the right tail [𝜇+n𝜎,∞)
    x_r = muK + n*sigmaK
    p_r = quad(rvK.pdf, x_r, rvK.ppf(0.9999999999))[0]
    # add together to get total probability in the tails
    p_tails = p_l + p_r
    print(f"Pr( K<{x_l} or K>{x_r} ) = {p_tails:.4f}")
muK = rvK.mean() # mean of the random variable rvK sigmaK = rvK.std() # standard deviation of rvK for n in [1, 2, 3]: # compute the probability in the left tail (-∞,𝜇-n𝜎] x_l = muK - n*sigmaK p_l = quad(rvK.pdf, rvK.ppf(0.0000000001), x_l)[0] # compute the probability in the right tail [𝜇+n𝜎,∞) x_r = muK + n*sigmaK p_r = quad(rvK.pdf, x_r, rvK.ppf(0.9999999999))[0] # add together to get total probability in the tails p_tails = p_l + p_r print(f"Pr( K<{x_l} or K>{x_r} ) = {p_tails:.4f}")
Pr( K<990.0 or K>1010.0 ) = 0.3173
Pr( K<980.0 or K>1020.0 ) = 0.0455
Pr( K<970.0 or K>1030.0 ) = 0.0027

The code below highlights the tails of the distribution and computes the sum of their probability.

In [53]:
Copied! 
from ministats import calc_prob_and_plot_tails

muK = rvK.mean()    # mean of the random variable rvK
sigmaK = rvK.std()  # standard deviation of rvK

n = 2  # number of standard deviations around the mean

# the distribution's left tail (-∞,𝜇-k𝜎]
x_l = muK - n*sigmaK
# the distribution's right tail [𝜇+k𝜎,∞)
x_r = muK + n*sigmaK
p_tails, _ = calc_prob_and_plot_tails(rvK, x_l, x_r, xlims=[960, 1040])

print(f"Pr( {{K<{x_l}}} ∪ {{K>{x_r}}} ) = {p_tails:.4f}")
from ministats import calc_prob_and_plot_tails muK = rvK.mean() # mean of the random variable rvK sigmaK = rvK.std() # standard deviation of rvK n = 2 # number of standard deviations around the mean # the distribution's left tail (-∞,𝜇-k𝜎] x_l = muK - n*sigmaK # the distribution's right tail [𝜇+k𝜎,∞) x_r = muK + n*sigmaK p_tails, _ = calc_prob_and_plot_tails(rvK, x_l, x_r, xlims=[960, 1040]) print(f"Pr( {{K<{x_l}}} ∪ {{K>{x_r}}} ) = {p_tails:.4f}")
Pr( {K<980.0} ∪ {K>1020.0} ) = 0.0455

Try changing the value of the variable k in the above code cell.

The above calculations leads us to an important rule of thumb: the values of the 5% tail of the distribution are $n=2$ standard deviations away from the mean (more precisely, we should use $n=1.96$ to get exactly 5%). We'll use this facts later in STATS to define a cutoff value for events that are unlikely to occur by chance.
